_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against WHO dracunculiasis eradication program data, CDC parasitology resources, and published clinical literature on Guinea worm disease. Note: 2026 may represent near-elimination status.

h1Title: Dracunculiasis (Guinea Worm Disease): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Dracunculiasis (Guinea worm disease) is a parasitic nematode infection caused by Dracunculus medinensis, the longest nematode to infect humans (adult females measure 60-120 cm in length). The infection is acquired exclusively by drinking water contaminated with copepods (water fleas) harboring D. medinensis larvae. After ingestion, larvae penetrate the stomach wall, mature in the abdominal cavity over approximately 3 months, and mate; pregnant females then migrate through subcutaneous tissue to the skin, most commonly of the lower extremities, where they form a painful blister and emerge — releasing thousands of larvae into freshwater sources when the ulcerated blister is submerged in water. An estimated 3.5 million annual cases occurred in the 1980s; aggressive WHO-led eradication efforts have reduced global incidence to fewer than 30 cases per year as of the 2020s, making dracunculiasis on the cusp of becoming the second human disease (after smallpox) to be eradicated. There is no drug treatment — the only therapy is gradual worm extraction by rolling it around a stick or small cylinder over days to weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: Peak of approximately 3.5 million annual cases in the mid-1980s; reduced to fewer than 30 cases per year by the 2020s through WHO-led eradication efforts; remaining cases are restricted to a small number of endemic countries in sub-Saharan Africa (Chad, Cameroon, Ethiopia, South Sudan, Sudan, Mali, and Angola)
keyStats.demographics: Equally affects males and females across all ages; cases are concentrated in rural subsistence farming communities without access to safe drinking water; no age immunity — all non-immune individuals are susceptible
keyStats.avgAge: Cases occur across all ages in endemic rural communities; children and adults who fetch and drink untreated water are at equal risk; the disease is most devastating in productive adults who cannot work during the weeks of incapacitation
keyStats.globalCases: Fewer than 30 cases per year globally as of the early 2020s — near-eradication status; in 2023, only 14 human cases were reported globally (provisional data from WHO); animal infections (dogs, cats) have become an important reservoir in some areas

definition: Dracunculiasis (ICD-10: B72) is a parasitic infection of humans caused by Dracunculus medinensis — the Guinea worm — a filarial nematode with a unique life cycle entirely dependent on copepods (microscopic crustaceans, Cyclops species) as intermediate hosts. Humans acquire infection by accidentally swallowing infected copepods in untreated drinking water. Larvae released in the human stomach penetrate the gastric wall and mature in the peritoneal and retroperitoneal cavities over approximately 3 months. After mating, male worms die and are absorbed; pregnant females (reaching 60-120 cm in length) migrate through subcutaneous and connective tissue toward the skin surface, most commonly of the lower extremities. As the worm approaches the skin surface, it forms a painful papule that develops into a blister — when the blister ruptures (often when the affected limb is immersed in water to soothe the pain), the female worm protrudes and releases thousands of first-stage larvae into the water, completing the life cycle. Humans are the only known definitive host — there is no animal reservoir (though dogs have been found infected in recent years, an emerging concern). There is no drug treatment — removal of the worm by slow traction (winding it around a stick or small cylinder) over days to weeks is the only intervention.

typesClassification.description: The approximately 3-month period between ingestion of infected copepods and emergence of the adult worm at the skin surface. Patients have no symptoms during this phase — there is no way to detect infection until the worm begins to emerge. This phase is the primary reason why mass screening is not feasible for dracunculiasis.

The adult female worm migrates to the skin surface, typically of the lower leg or foot, forming a painful papule that evolves into a fluid-filled blister over 1-3 days. When the blister ruptures (often after contact with water), the white worm (1-2 mm in diameter) emerges through the ulcer, releasing larvae. This phase causes severe pain, secondary bacterial infection of the ulcer, and incapacitation for weeks to months.

typesClassification.type: Secondary bacterial infection of Guinea worm ulcer
typesClassification.description: The most common complication of dracunculiasis — the ulcer created by the emerging worm is rapidly colonized by skin bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes, and others), causing cellulitis, abscess formation, and systemic infection. Secondary infection is the leading cause of morbidity and prolonged incapacitation in dracunculiasis.

typesClassification.type: Guinea worm infection in animals (emerging concern)
typesClassification.description: In recent years, D. medinensis infection has been documented in dogs (and rarely cats) in Chad, Cameroon, and other endemic areas. Canine dracunculiasis is a concern for eradication because dogs may serve as a reservoir, contaminating water sources even when human cases are eliminated. This complicates the final stages of eradication.

primarySymptoms: Painful papule on the lower leg or foot progressing to a blister — the initial cutaneous sign of dracunculiasis; a raised, red papule forms over the site where the adult female worm will emerge, progressing to a fluid-filled blister over 1-3 days; the blister is intensely painful and itchy.

Ulceration and worm protrusion from the skin — when the blister ruptures (often spontaneously or after contact with water), the adult female worm (appearing as a white thread-like structure, 60-120 cm in length, 1-2 mm in diameter) emerges through the ulcer, releasing larvae when submerged in water.

Severe burning pain at the site of worm emergence — the ulcerated skin and subcutis at the worm emergence site is exquisitely tender; patients describe a burning, searing pain that is often worse at night; the pain incapacitates patients and prevents them from working or walking.

Secondary bacterial infection of the Guinea worm ulcer — the open ulcer is rapidly colonized by skin bacteria; signs include increasing redness, warmth, swelling, purulent discharge, fever, and regional lymphadenopathy; untreated, it can progress to cellulitis, abscess, or even sepsis.

Incapacitation and inability to walk or work — the combination of severe pain and secondary infection in the lower extremity makes ambulation extremely painful or impossible; patients may be bedridden for 2-8 weeks during the emergence phase; agricultural workers in endemic regions lose entire planting or harvest seasons.

Allergic reactions and systemic symptoms during worm emergence — some patients develop low-grade fever, generalized pruritus, urticaria, and nausea during the emergence phase, possibly from worm antigens released during larval release.
Swelling, redness, and induration around the emergence site — the periwound area is inflamed with significant edema; cellulitis is common and may spread up the limb.

Contractures and disability from scarring — after the worm emerges and the ulcer heals, dense scarring at the emergence site can cause contractures of the ankle or foot, limiting mobility long-term; repeated infections compound disability.
earlyWarningSigns: Development of a painful papule/blister on the lower leg in a patient from an endemic region who has consumed untreated water — in endemic areas, this presentation is essentially diagnostic of dracunculiasis; patients should be isolated from water sources to prevent larval contamination

Fever, malaise, and localized pain/redness of the lower extremity in a patient from an endemic region — secondary bacterial infection is common and may be the first clinical clue that a Guinea worm is emerging
Unhealed ulcer of the lower leg with a visible worm — the adult female worm is visible as a white thread-like structure emerging from the ulcer; this is pathognomonic

Seasonal presentation in endemic areas — dracunculiasis cases cluster during the rainy season (when people drink more surface water) in most endemic areas; awareness of local seasonal patterns helps identify cases early

Household clustering of cases — multiple members of a household or village may develop Guinea worm lesions simultaneously from drinking from the same contaminated water source; this clustering is a diagnostic clue
emergencySigns: Septicemia from secondary bacterial infection — high fever, rigors, hypotension, and altered mental status in a patient with a Guinea worm ulcer indicate bacterial sepsis requiring emergent IV antibiotics and hospitalization

Tetanus complicating Guinea worm ulcer — contamination of the ulcer with Clostridium tetani spores can cause tetanus, particularly in unvaccinated individuals; lockjaw, opisthotonus, and muscle rigidity are medical emergencies requiring tetanus immunoglobulin and supportive care

Severe cellulitis with rapidly spreading redness and systemic toxicity — spreading cellulitis from secondary infection can cause severe systemic illness; requires IV antibiotics and possibly surgical drainage

Multiple concurrent Guinea worms emerging simultaneously — a patient with 3-5 worms emerging at once faces compounded disability, pain, and infection risk; this indicates heavy exposure and requires aggressive wound care and isolation

Disability from ankle/foot contractures after repeated infections — repeated dracunculiasis infections over years cause progressive scarring and contracture of the ankle and foot joints; this is a chronic emergency for patients' livelihoods

causes.cause: Ingestion of Dracunculus medinensis larvae in contaminated drinking water
causes.description: The sole route of human infection — humans drink untreated freshwater containing copepods (Cyclops species, water fleas) that have ingested D. medinensis larvae. Inside the human gut, the copepod dies and releases larvae that penetrate the gastric and intestinal wall, reaching the peritoneal and retroperitoneal cavities where they mature over approximately 3 months. The water drunk is typically from surface sources (ponds, rivers, shallow wells) that have been contaminated by humans with emerging Guinea worms soaking their legs in water.

causes.cause: Drinking from unprotected surface water sources (ponds, rivers, shallow wells)
causes.description: In endemic rural areas, the primary risk is consumption of untreated surface water from ponds, slow-moving rivers, hand-dug wells, and other unprotected sources. The water contains infected copepods (visible only under a microscope). Community water sources contaminated by a single infected person can infect dozens of other individuals who drink from the same source.

causes.cause: Contamination of water sources by humans with emerging Guinea worms
causes.description: When a person with an emerging Guinea worm ulcer soaks their leg in water to relieve pain or cool the wound, the female worm releases thousands of larvae into the water, infecting copepods. This single act can contaminate an entire water source, creating a community-wide risk. Patient isolation from water sources is a critical intervention for eradication.

causes.cause: Absence of safe drinking water and water filtration in endemic communities
causes.description: The root cause of dracunculiasis is lack of access to safe drinking water — communities without piped water or protected wells depend on surface water contaminated with copepods. Water filtration (using cloth filters or commercial filter cloths with pores below 150 micrometers) prevents copepod ingestion and is the primary individual prevention strategy.

causes.cause: Emerging animal reservoir (dogs as reservoir hosts)
causes.description: In Chad and other countries, D. medinensis has been found infecting dogs at rates higher than human cases in some areas. Dogs may serve as an unintended reservoir, contaminating water sources even after human cases are eliminated. This is a critical emerging challenge for the final stages of eradication.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to endemic rural areas in sub-Saharan Africa
riskFactors.description: All remaining endemic countries are in sub-Saharan Africa — Chad, Cameroon, Ethiopia, South Sudan, Sudan, Mali, and Angola are the primary remaining foci. Travelers to these areas who drink untreated surface water are at risk.

Drinking from ponds, rivers, hand-dug wells, or other unprotected surface water sources is the primary behavioral risk factor. Using a cloth filter to strain water before drinking prevents copepod ingestion.

riskFactors.factor: Absence of safe piped water supply or protected wells
riskFactors.description: The fundamental root cause of dracunculiasis — communities without access to safe drinking water must drink from surface sources contaminated with infected copepods. Provision of safe water is the ultimate solution for eradication.

riskFactors.factor: Rural subsistence farming communities without water treatment
riskFactors.description: The disease affects the most marginalized rural populations — subsistence farmers who cannot afford bottled water or filtration systems and who depend on local surface water for drinking.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of health education about water filtration and disease transmission
riskFactors.description: Communities unaware of the transmission mechanism cannot implement effective prevention — health education about water filtration, not drinking untreated water, and not soaking emergent worm sites in water are the key behavioral interventions.

affectedDemographics: Dracunculiasis affects all ages and both sexes equally in endemic areas — no age or sex immunity; all non-immune individuals who drink contaminated water are susceptible

The burden is concentrated in the most impoverished rural communities in sub-Saharan Africa — the disease is a marker of extreme poverty and lack of water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) infrastructure

Agricultural workers bear a disproportionate economic burden — the disease incapacitates productive adults during the peak agricultural season, causing food insecurity and economic loss at the household level
Children may miss school during the emergence season if they are the ones with Guinea worm lesions or if they are the ones caring for incapacitated family members
The near-elimination of dracunculiasis has left only a handful of endemic villages, primarily in South Sudan, Chad, and Cameroon — the remaining cases are geographically highly clustered
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of dracunculiasis is primarily clinical — a painful papule progressing to a blister and ulcer on the lower extremity, with the emergence of a visible white thread-like worm (60-120 cm long) from the ulcer, in a patient from an endemic area, is essentially pathognomonic. Laboratory confirmation is rarely needed for clinical cases. In the pre-emergence phase (first 3 months after ingestion), there is no way to diagnose dracunculiasis — infection is detectable only when the adult worm emerges. Radiographic identification of calcified worms in subcutaneous tissue has been documented but is not used clinically. Stool or urine examination is NOT useful — larvae are released through the skin ulcer, not the GI or urinary tract. The WHO eradication program uses a standardized case definition: a person with a skin lesion with emerging Guinea worm, or a lesion that is suspected to be Guinea worm (not yet emerged). Serology (ELISA for D. medinensis antibodies) is not used clinically but has been used in prevalence surveys. The key public health action upon identifying a case is immediate notification to the WHO Guinea Worm Eradication Program through national health authorities.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical diagnosis (visual identification of emerging worm)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic method — the adult female Guinea worm (60-120 cm long, 1-2 mm diameter, white thread-like) emerging from an ulcerated blister on the lower extremity is pathognomonic. No laboratory test is required for clinical diagnosis in endemic areas.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination of the lower extremities (and occasionally arms or torso); the worm is directly visible to the naked eye.

diagnosticTests.test: WHO standardized case definition reporting (active surveillance)
diagnosticTests.purpose: For eradication program surveillance, the WHO case definition is used: any individual with a skin lesion through which a Guinea worm is emerging, or a lesion that has not yet emerged but is suspected to be Guinea worm (based on location, seasonality, and endemic status). All suspected and confirmed cases are reported to national programs and the WHO.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized reporting forms submitted to district health authorities and the WHO Guinea Worm Eradication Program.

diagnosticTests.test: Microscopic examination of water for infected copepods (environmental surveillance)
diagnosticTests.purpose: As part of the eradication program, water sources in endemic areas are monitored for the presence of infected copepods — water is filtered through paper and the filter is examined microscopically for D. medinensis larvae inside copepods. Positive environmental samples indicate ongoing transmission in that area.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Water samples collected from drinking sources and filtered through fine-mesh paper; examined under microscopy. Results within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serology has been used for population prevalence surveys in endemic areas — detects antibodies against D. medinensis in people who have been infected (including those whose worm has not yet emerged). Not useful for clinical diagnosis but useful for program surveillance to identify hidden transmission.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for ELISA; results from reference parasitology labs in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: X-ray or ultrasound identification of calcified or migrating worms
diagnosticTests.purpose: Calcified Guinea worms can be identified on X-ray as long, thin radiopaque structures in subcutaneous tissue — a finding occasionally seen in surveys of previously infected individuals. Ultrasound can identify live worms migrating in subcutaneous tissue.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray of affected limb; results immediately. Ultrasound with high-frequency linear probe over migration tract; results immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Bacterial culture of ulcer discharge (for secondary infection)
diagnosticTests.purpose: For Guinea worm ulcers with signs of secondary bacterial infection (increasing erythema, purulent discharge, fever), culture of the discharge identifies the causative organism (most commonly Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes) and guides antibiotic selection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Swab of ulcer discharge for bacterial culture and sensitivity; results in 2-5 days.

treatmentOverview: There is no drug treatment, vaccine, or specific anthelmintic for dracunculiasis. The only intervention is slow mechanical extraction of the worm by daily wound care and gradual traction. The affected limb is cleaned, the worm is gently pulled out 2-3 cm each day (by winding it around a stick or small cylinder), and the wound is dressed with clean bandages. The worm's body is fragile — breaking it during extraction causes severe inflammatory reactions and can leave the remaining portion unable to be extracted. Full extraction takes between 1 day and several weeks. Secondary bacterial infection is treated with oral or IV antibiotics (depending on severity) — common organisms are Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Streptococcus pyogenes. Wound care with daily cleaning and bandaging reduces secondary infection risk. Tetanus prophylaxis (tetanus toxoid vaccine) is given to all patients with Guinea worm ulcers. Analgesics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) manage pain. Patients must be isolated from water sources during the emergence phase to prevent contaminating drinking water with larvae. The definitive long-term intervention is provision of safe drinking water to eliminate the transmission route.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical worm extraction (gradual traction over days to weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: The sole therapeutic intervention for dracunculiasis — the emerging worm is gently pulled out 2-3 cm per day by winding it around a stick, matchstick, or small cylinder. Daily wound cleaning and bandaging accompanies traction. Full extraction may take 1 day to several weeks. Breaking the worm causes severe inflammation and prevents complete extraction — gentle, persistent traction is essential.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful extraction of the intact worm is the goal — broken worms cannot be fully extracted and cause prolonged inflammation. Most patients complete extraction within 1-3 weeks with daily care.

medicalTreatments.name: Wound care with daily cleaning and bandaging
medicalTreatments.description: Daily wound cleaning with antiseptic solution (povidone-iodine, chlorhexidine), removal of debris, and application of clean dry bandages reduces secondary bacterial infection and promotes healing. The wound is kept clean and dry between dressing changes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective at reducing secondary bacterial infection and accelerating healing; combined with antibiotic treatment when infection is present.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics for secondary bacterial infection (clindamycin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, TMP-SMX)
medicalTreatments.description: Secondary bacterial infection is treated with oral antibiotics — the most common pathogens are Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA in some areas) and Streptococcus pyogenes. Amoxicillin-clavulanate, clindamycin, or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole are commonly used. Severe infections require IV antibiotics and hospitalization.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotics reduce the morbidity from secondary infection — cellulitis, abscess, and sepsis are prevented or treated with appropriate antimicrobial coverage.

medicalTreatments.name: Tetanus prophylaxis (tetanus toxoid vaccine) for all patients with Guinea worm ulcers
medicalTreatments.description: All patients with Guinea worm ulcers should receive tetanus toxoid vaccine — the contaminated ulcer is a risk for Clostridium tetani infection. Tetanus toxoid (0.5 mL IM) is given to all patients regardless of vaccination history; tetanus immunoglobulin (250 IU IM) is given to unvaccinated or inadequately vaccinated patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tetanus prophylaxis prevents a potentially fatal complication of dracunculiasis; all patients with Guinea worm ulcers should receive it.

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) for pain management
medicalTreatments.description: Pain from the emerging worm and secondary infection is managed with acetaminophen (500-1000 mg every 4-6 hours) or NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-800 mg every 6-8 hours). Topical analgesics (lidocaine gel) may provide temporary relief for the ulcerated skin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Analgesics improve quality of life during the weeks of incapacitation but do not alter the natural course of the infection.

medicalTreatments.name: IV antibiotics and hospitalization for severe secondary infection
medicalTreatments.description: Patients with severe cellulitis, abscess, or sepsis from secondary bacterial infection require IV antibiotics (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam, or clindamycin alone for less severe cases) and hospitalization. Septicemia from Guinea worm ulcers is a medical emergency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IV antibiotics and supportive care resolve septicemia and severe soft tissue infections in most patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision of the worm (in rare cases where traction has failed)
surgicalOptions.description: If the worm cannot be extracted by gentle traction (e.g., it has broken, or migration is in a deep location), surgical excision may be required. However, surgery is rarely needed — gentle traction is effective in over 95% of cases. The wound is already open, so the intervention is limited to careful extraction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical extraction resolves the case when traction has failed; most worms are successfully extracted without surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Metronidazole (for secondary bacterial infection)
alternativeTreatments.description: Metronidazole is sometimes used in combination with other antibiotics for severe polymicrobial wound infections associated with Guinea worm ulcers — it covers anaerobes that may colonize the wound.

whySeeSpecialist: In endemic areas, community health workers trained by the Guinea Worm Eradication Program are the primary caregivers for dracunculiasis — they provide wound care, teach water filtration, and report cases to the program. Patients with severe secondary infection, sepsis, or complicated cases require referral to a health center or hospital for IV antibiotics and hospitalization. Infectious disease and tropical medicine specialists at referral centers in endemic countries provide care for complicated cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic countries, the Guinea Worm Eradication Program (run by national health ministries with WHO coordination) trains community health workers in Guinea worm management. For complicated cases (severe secondary infection, sepsis), seek a health center with wound care capacity and IV antibiotics. The eradication program maintains a network of surveillance and care across all endemic countries.
preventionStrategies: Drinking only filtered or boiled water — using a cloth filter (pores below 150 micrometers) or commercial water filter to strain water before drinking prevents copepod ingestion; boiling water for 1 minute kills copepods and larvae; this is the single most effective individual prevention measure

Providing safe piped water or protected wells — the definitive long-term solution for dracunculiasis is elimination of the need to drink untreated surface water; the WHO eradication program prioritizes water source improvement in endemic villages

Patient isolation from water sources — patients with emerging Guinea worms must NOT soak their ulcers in drinking water sources; they should be cared for in a dry area, and bathing water must be disposed of in a latrine or dry ground away from water sources

Health education in endemic communities — teaching communities about the life cycle of Guinea worm, the importance of water filtration, and the prohibition against soaking wounds in drinking water is a core intervention of the eradication program

Active surveillance and case containment — finding and containing all cases within 24 hours of worm emergence prevents contamination of water sources; the eradication program relies on community-based surveillance volunteers to identify and report cases
lifestyleModifications: Filter all drinking water using a cloth filter — any surface water must be filtered through a clean cloth with pores small enough to catch copepods (filter cloth provided by the Guinea Worm Eradication Program); this is the single most important behavioral change

Do not soak the Guinea worm ulcer in any water source used for drinking — bathing water must be collected in a container, not from a river or pond; soiled water must be disposed of in a latrine or dry ground away from water sources
Seek care from a trained community health worker immediately upon noticing a Guinea worm blister — early wound care and patient isolation prevents secondary infection and water contamination
Cover water storage containers — covered containers prevent copepods from entering stored drinking water; a simple lid on a water pot eliminates the risk of copepod contamination during storage

Do not return to drinking from the same water source once a case has been identified in the community — the source may be contaminated; wait for the eradication program to treat the water source or provide an alternative

recommended: Clean, filtered water — filtered water is the most critical dietary intervention for dracunculiasis; all drinking water must be filtered through a cloth filter or boiled before consumption
High-protein diet for wound healing — lean meats, fish, legumes; supports healing of the ulcerated skin and recovery from secondary bacterial infection
Vitamin C-rich foods — citrus fruits, bell peppers; supports immune function and wound healing
avoid: Unfiltered surface water from ponds, rivers, or hand-dug wells — this is the exclusive source of dracunculiasis transmission; absolutely no untreated surface water should be consumed

Bathing or washing in drinking water sources — patients with Guinea worm ulcers must avoid soaking their legs in any water source used for drinking by the community; contaminated water disperses larvae

exerciseGuidelines: Patients with active Guinea worm emergence of the lower extremity are significantly disabled by pain and secondary infection — ambulation is often impossible for weeks. Once the worm is fully extracted and the ulcer is healed (typically 3-8 weeks), normal activity can resume. Patients with residual ankle/foot contractures from scarring may need physical therapy for range of motion exercises.
dailyManagement: Filter all drinking water without exception — cloth filter use for every drink of water is non-negotiable; no drinking from open surface sources
Care for the wound daily with clean water and antiseptic — wound care by trained health workers; daily gentle traction on the worm; clean dry bandaging between sessions
Keep the wound absolutely dry — do not soak the ulcer in water; bathing water must be from a clean container, and soiled water must be disposed of away from drinking water sources
Report the case to the nearest health worker or Guinea Worm Eradication Program surveillance volunteer — immediate reporting is critical for case containment and water source investigation
Watch for signs of secondary infection (fever, spreading redness, pus) — secondary bacterial infection requires antibiotics; seek care promptly if these develop
prognosis: Dracunculiasis is not fatal in most cases — the infection causes severe morbidity and incapacitation but rarely causes death. With appropriate wound care, full recovery occurs within 3-8 weeks in most patients. The primary determinants of poor outcome are secondary bacterial infection (septicemia, cellulitis) and tetanus — these complications can cause death if not treated promptly. Patients who develop contractures from scarring may have permanent mobility limitations. With near-eradication status (fewer than 30 cases per year globally), the prognosis of the disease is more relevant as a public health problem than as an individual clinical entity — the remaining cases are confined to a few highly marginalized communities.
recoveryTimeline: The worm emergence phase lasts 1 day to several weeks — the worm is typically fully extracted within 2-4 weeks of emergence with daily gentle traction. The ulcer heals over 2-6 weeks after worm extraction with proper wound care. Secondary bacterial infections, if treated promptly, resolve within 1-2 weeks. Complete healing without disability is expected in most patients who receive proper wound care. Patients who develop contractures from scarring may have permanent joint limitation.
complications: Septicemia from secondary bacterial infection — the leading cause of death from dracunculiasis; bacterial pathogens (S. aureus, S. pyogenes, gram-negative organisms) from the ulcer enter the bloodstream causing sepsis; requires emergent IV antibiotics

Tetanus — contamination of the Guinea worm ulcer with Clostridium tetani spores causes tetanus (lockjaw, rigidity, opisthotonus); mortality is high even with treatment; tetanus toxoid prophylaxis is critical for all patients

Cellulitis and soft tissue abscess — secondary bacterial infection of the soft tissue surrounding the Guinea worm ulcer causes spreading erythema, warmth, pain, and purulence; requires antibiotics and possibly surgical drainage

Chronic leg ulcers — some ulcers persist for months after worm extraction due to chronic infection or foreign body reaction to worm fragments; require prolonged wound care and possible surgical debridement

Joint contractures and permanent disability — dense scarring at the site of repeated Guinea worm emergence can cause flexion contractures of the ankle and foot, limiting mobility long-term; this is a chronic disability for patients with recurrent infections
Guinea worm arthritis — spread of infection into adjacent joints from a Guinea worm ulcer overlying a joint can cause septic arthritis, requiring IV antibiotics and possibly surgical drainage

supportResources.name: WHO Guinea Worm Eradication Program
supportResources.description: WHO dracunculiasis eradication program — global surveillance data, eradication status, and program resources for endemic countries.

supportResources.name: CDC Parasitic Diseases — Dracunculiasis
supportResources.description: CDC information on dracunculiasis for US clinicians and travelers — transmission, prevention, and clinical management.

supportResources.name: The Carter Center Guinea Worm Program
supportResources.description: The Carter Center has led the Guinea worm eradication program since 1986 — supports surveillance, water filtration distribution, health education, and case containment in all remaining endemic countries.

supportResources.name: Greenaway C. Dracunculus medinensis infection. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2022.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive review of dracunculiasis — epidemiology, life cycle, clinical presentation, and management.

supportResources.name: Cairncross S, et al. Why dracunculiasis eradication is now within reach. BMJ. 2022.
supportResources.description: Analysis of the near-eradication status of dracunculiasis and remaining challenges, including animal reservoirs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous Larva Migrans
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CLM causes serpiginous, pruritic, erythematous tracks in the superficial epidermis from animal hookworm larvae — it does not form a blister with an emerging worm and is not associated with water ingestion. The lesions of CLM are itchy migration tracks, not a single emerging nematode.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous Leishmaniasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cutaneous leishmaniasis causes ulcerating papules or nodules on exposed skin areas but does not have an emerging worm; the ulcers have a characteristic 'volcano crater' appearance and have a sandy/gritty texture. Leishmaniasis is diagnosed by slit-skin smear or biopsy, not by visual identification of a worm.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous Myiasis (maggot infestation)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myiasis is infestation of tissues by fly larvae — maggots can emerge from skin ulcers or subcutaneous nodules, superficially resembling Guinea worm. However, myiasis larvae are typically shorter, thicker, and have visible segmentation and mouth hooks; they emerge from tissue rather than from an ulcerating blister, and there is no history of water ingestion.

relatedConditions.name: Ancylostomiasis (Hookworm)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are nematode parasites acquired from environmental exposure in tropical regions, but the life cycles, transmission routes, and clinical presentations are entirely different. Dracunculus is transmitted by water (copepods), while Ancylostoma is transmitted by larval skin penetration from soil. Both can cause significant morbidity in endemic populations.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both Strongyloides stercoralis and Dracunculus medinensis can be acquired from environmental sources and both can emerge from skin surfaces, but Strongyloides emerges as larvae from the perianal skin (not from blisters on the lower extremities) and is diagnosed by stool examination, not by visual worm emergence.

relatedConditions.name: Onchocerciasis (River Blindness)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both dracunculiasis and onchocerciasis have been targeted for eradication/elimination by WHO and both are associated with tropical rural communities lacking safe water; both have vector or water-related transmission dynamics, making them part of the same group of neglected tropical diseases targeted by WASH-based interventions.

faqs.question: What is dracunculiasis?
faqs.answer: Dracunculiasis (Guinea worm disease) is a parasitic infection caused by Dracunculus medinensis — a nematode worm acquired by drinking water contaminated with infected copepods (water fleas). The adult female worm (up to 120 cm long) emerges from a blister in the skin, usually on the leg.

faqs.question: How do you get Guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: Guinea worm disease is acquired by drinking freshwater containing infected copepods (microscopic water fleas). The larvae inside the copepods are released in the stomach, penetrate the gut wall, mature inside the body, and after 3 months, the female worm emerges through the skin.

faqs.question: Is Guinea worm disease curable?
faqs.answer: There is no drug treatment for Guinea worm disease. The only intervention is slow mechanical extraction of the worm by winding it around a stick over days to weeks. With proper wound care, the ulcer heals within a few weeks after the worm is fully extracted.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: Three months after drinking contaminated water, a painful blister forms on the lower leg or foot. When the blister ruptures, the female Guinea worm (appearing as a white thread) emerges through the ulcer. This causes severe burning pain, secondary bacterial infection, and incapacitation for weeks.

faqs.question: How is Guinea worm disease prevented?
faqs.answer: The primary prevention is filtering all drinking water through a fine cloth to remove copepods, or boiling water for 1 minute. Patients with emerging worms must not soak their ulcers in drinking water sources — this contaminates the water with larvae. Providing safe water (piped water or protected wells) is the long-term solution.

faqs.question: Is Guinea worm disease fatal?
faqs.answer: Guinea worm disease is rarely fatal — death occurs in fewer than 1% of cases. Fatal cases are due to secondary bacterial infections (sepsis, tetanus) rather than the worm itself. With proper wound care and antibiotics, most patients recover completely.

faqs.question: How many cases of Guinea worm disease are there?
faqs.answer: There were approximately 3.5 million cases per year in the mid-1980s. Through the WHO-led eradication program, this has been reduced to fewer than 30 cases per year as of the 2020s — making dracunculiasis the second human disease (after smallpox) on the cusp of eradication.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for Guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: There is no drug treatment. The worm is slowly extracted by daily wound care and gentle traction — winding it 2-3 cm per day around a stick. Breaking the worm causes severe inflammation. Secondary bacterial infection is treated with antibiotics. Tetanus prophylaxis is given to all patients.

faqs.question: Where is Guinea worm disease found?
faqs.answer: As of the 2020s, cases are restricted to a small number of countries in sub-Saharan Africa — primarily Chad, Cameroon, Ethiopia, South Sudan, Sudan, Mali, and Angola. Most endemic areas are remote rural villages without access to safe drinking water.

faqs.question: Can dogs get Guinea worm disease?
faqs.answer: Yes — in Chad, Cameroon, and other countries, dogs have been found infected with D. medinensis at rates that sometimes exceed human cases. Dogs may serve as an animal reservoir, contaminating water sources even as human cases decline. This is a major challenge for final eradication.

faqs.question: Why is it called Guinea worm?
faqs.answer: The name 'Guinea worm' reflects the historical geographic origin of the disease — the infection was described in 'Guinea' (West Africa) by European observers in the 17th century. The worm (Dracunculus medinensis) has been known since ancient Egyptian times — it is depicted in Egyptian medical papyri dating back 3,500 years.
